Preventive maintenance — the owner may sign off
Nothing is needed if the new radio turns on and functions
practice.explanation: Like-for-like TSO replacement done per the manufacturer's manual is maintenance, not a major alteration. The required documentation is a §43.9-compliant logbook entry. Pilot-owner preventive maintenance is not the applicable category, and documentation is always required for maintenance work.

preassessment.question: Is replacing a TSO part with another TSO part generally a major alteration?
choices: Generally no
Yes — always major
Requires an STC
Is preventive maintenance

questions.learningObjective: Identify what the acronym STC stands for
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that STC stands for Supplemental Type Certificate — an FAA-issued approval that authorizes a major design change to a type-certificated aircraft, engine, propeller, or appliance.

flashcard.definition: STC stands for Supplemental Type Certificate. It is an FAA-issued approval that authorizes a major design change to a type-certificated product (aircraft, engine, propeller, or appliance). The STC provides the approved data needed to make and document the change. STCs are owned by their holders and must be referenced when installations are made per their approval.
flashcard.analogy: An STC is like a formally approved 'mod kit' — the FAA has reviewed the design change and certified that it produces an airworthy result when installed as specified.

jeopardy.question: This is what the acronym STC stands for.
choices: Standard Technical Certificate
Specialized Tool Certification
Standard Testing Code
Supplemental Type Certificate
jeopardy.explanation: STC is Supplemental Type Certificate — the FAA's approval for a major design change to a type-certificated product.

multipleChoice.question: What does the acronym STC stand for?
choices: Standard Technical Certificate
Specialized Tool Certification
Standard Testing Code
Supplemental Type Certificate
multipleChoice.explanation: STC means Supplemental Type Certificate. It is an FAA approval for a major design change to a type-certificated aircraft, engine, propeller, or appliance. STC-installed modifications use the STC as approved data and typically require Form 337 documentation.

practice.question: A technician plans to install a new avionics package that the vendor describes as 'STC-approved for Cessna 172 variants.' What does the STC designation tell the technician?
choices: That any standard tools will work for the installation
That the avionics are cheaper than alternatives
That the avionics are certified only for experimental aircraft

That the FAA has issued a Supplemental Type Certificate for the installation — the STC provides the approved data needed to install the package on the listed aircraft, and the technician must use the STC's installation manual and required documentation
practice.explanation: An STC-approved installation means the FAA has issued an STC authorizing the modification for the listed aircraft. The STC's approved data (installation manual, drawings, limitations) must be followed exactly. Pricing and experimental classification are unrelated to STC approval.

preassessment.question: What does STC stand for?
choices: Standard Technical Certificate
Specialized Tool Certification
Standard Testing Code
Supplemental Type Certificate

questions.learningObjective: Identify the typical recurrent training interval for Part 145 repair station personnel
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that most Part 145 training programs require recurrent training for repair station personnel at intervals not exceeding 24 months, with the specific interval and content defined in the station's FAA-accepted training program manual.

flashcard.definition: Most Part 145 training programs specify recurrent training for repair-station personnel at intervals not exceeding 24 months, with the specifics (subjects, evaluation criteria, documentation) defined in the station's FAA-accepted training program. Each repair station's program manual is the authoritative source for its own personnel.
flashcard.analogy: Two-year recurrent training is the common default — the specifics always come from the station's own approved training program.

jeopardy.question: According to most Part 145 training programs, this is the typical interval for recurrent training of repair station personnel.
choices: Not exceeding every 6 months
Not exceeding every 60 months
Only upon hire — no recurrent training required
Not exceeding every 24 months
jeopardy.explanation: A 24-month cycle is typical for Part 145 recurrent training. Shorter (6-month) intervals are uncommon. 60 months is too long to keep personnel current. Recurrent training is required — initial training alone does not satisfy Part 145 requirements.

multipleChoice.question: How often must repair-station personnel typically undergo recurrent training per most Part 145 training programs?
choices: At least every 6 months
At least every 60 months
Only upon initial hire — no recurrent training is required
At intervals not exceeding 24 months
multipleChoice.explanation: A 24-month recurrent training cycle is typical for Part 145 stations. The specific interval and subject content are defined in each station's FAA-accepted training program manual. Personnel can check their own training records against the station's program to confirm compliance.

practice.question: A technician at a Part 145 repair station notes that their last formal training was 26 months ago. What does most Part 145 guidance say about this?
choices: No concern — training is a one-time initial event
The technician must be immediately terminated
Training is only required for pilots, not technicians

The technician is likely overdue for recurrent training, which most Part 145 programs specify at intervals not exceeding 24 months. The station's training program manual is the authoritative source for the exact requirement; the technician should consult the manager and complete the required training
practice.explanation: Most Part 145 programs specify recurrent training at intervals not exceeding 24 months. The technician should consult the station's training program manual to confirm and complete the required training. Termination is not the remedy for a training lapse — completing the training is. Training is explicitly required for repair-station technical personnel, not only for pilots.

preassessment.question: How often must Part 145 personnel typically undergo recurrent training?
choices: Every 6 months
Every 60 months
Only upon hire
Every 24 months

questions.learningObjective: Describe the primary purpose of a Part 145 repair station's capability list
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that a Part 145 repair station's capability list identifies the specific articles (by make, model, or other description) the station is approved to perform maintenance, preventive maintenance, or alterations on, under its ratings and within its approved procedures.

flashcard.definition: A capability list under Part 145 identifies the specific articles (by make, model, or other description) the repair station is authorized to perform maintenance, preventive maintenance, or alterations on under its certificate ratings. Items on the capability list must be supported by required documentation, personnel, tooling, and technical data.
flashcard.analogy: The capability list is the repair station's menu of work it is approved to do — adding a new item requires the right staff, tools, and data.

jeopardy.question: This is the primary purpose of a Part 145 repair station's capability list.
choices: To identify the station's employee roster
To identify the specific articles (by make, model, or description) the station is authorized to perform maintenance on under its ratings
To list the station's pricing for each job
To record the aircraft the station has worked on historically
jeopardy.explanation: The capability list identifies what the station is certified to work on — by make/model or description. Employee rosters, pricing, and work history are separate records that do not constitute the capability list.

multipleChoice.question: What is the main purpose of a Part 145 repair station's capability list?
choices: To identify the station's current employees
To identify the specific articles the station is authorized to perform
To list the station's pricing for repair jobs
To record the aircraft the station has worked on in the past
multipleChoice.explanation: The capability list is a regulatory document that identifies which articles the station is certified to work on. Adding an item requires demonstrating the station has the personnel, tooling, data, and facilities to support it. Employee rosters, pricing, and historical logs are separate records.

practice.question: A repair station's capability list shows a specific radio model as 'within capabilities,' but the latest manual revision for that radio is not currently available in the shop. Can the station legally work on that radio?
choices: Yes, because the radio is on the capability list
No — capability includes the requirement that current technical data be available. Without the latest manual revision, the station cannot perform the work until the current data is obtained
Yes, if the technician is experienced with the model
Yes, with a note in the work order that the data is out of date
practice.explanation: Being on the capability list is necessary but not sufficient. The station must also have current technical data available. Without the latest manual revision, the technical-data requirement is not met, and the station cannot perform work per its own accepted procedures and Part 145 obligations. Current data must be obtained before proceeding.

preassessment.question: What is the purpose of a Part 145 capability list?
choices: List employees
Identify specific articles
List pricing
Record historical work

questions.learningObjective: Identify who is responsible for ensuring current technical data is available and used in a Part 145 repair station
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that under Part 145, the repair station is responsible for ensuring current technical data is available and used during maintenance, preventive maintenance, and alteration work — specifically, the station management and designated personnel (per the accepted procedures manual) must maintain current data as a condition of the station's certificate.

flashcard.definition: Under 14 CFR Part 145, the repair station itself is responsible for ensuring current technical data is available and used during maintenance. The station's accepted procedures manual designates specific personnel (typically including the Accountable Manager and Chief Inspector or equivalent) to maintain current manuals, service bulletins, and advisory data. Using outdated data violates Part 145 requirements.
flashcard.analogy: Current technical data is a continuous requirement — the station cannot do work correctly without it, and its maintenance is a responsibility of the station itself, not of a single technician.

jeopardy.question: Under Part 145, this party is responsible for ensuring that current technical data is available and used during avionics maintenance.
choices: The repair station itself (through its designated personnel, as defined in the station's accepted procedures manual)
The individual aircraft owner
The FAA
The part manufacturer alone
jeopardy.explanation: Part 145 places the responsibility on the repair station. The station's accepted procedures manual designates specific personnel for this responsibility. The FAA oversees; it does not maintain individual stations' technical data. Part manufacturers publish the data but do not ensure it is in use at a specific station.

multipleChoice.question: Under 14 CFR Part 145, who is responsible for ensuring that current technical data is available and used during avionics maintenance?
choices: The repair station itself, through the designated
The individual aircraft owner
The FAA inspector assigned to the station
The part manufacturer alone
multipleChoice.explanation: The repair station bears this responsibility under Part 145. Specific personnel (typically the Accountable Manager and Chief Inspector, or equivalent positions in the station's procedures manual) are designated to ensure current data is on hand. The FAA provides oversight, not data management. Manufacturers publish the data but cannot ensure its use at individual stations.

practice.question: A technician at a Part 145 station finds that the current service bulletin for a GPS model is missing from the shop's technical data library. What is the correct course of action?
choices: Stop work until the current service bulletin is obtained. Per Part 145, the repair station is responsible for ensuring current data is available; the technician should notify the designated personnel (Accountable Manager, Chief Inspector, or as specified in the procedures manual) so the missing data can be obtained before work continues
Proceed with the work and note the missing data in the logbook
Search online forums for the missing information
Improvise based on experience
practice.explanation: Part 145 requires current data to be in use. The correct action is to stop work, notify the designated personnel responsible for data currency, obtain the current service bulletin, and resume work. Proceeding without current data, improvising, or using unofficial sources all violate Part 145 and risk improper work.

preassessment.question: Who ensures current technical data is available in a Part 145 shop?
choices: The repair station itself
Aircraft owner
The FAA
The part manufacturer

questions.learningObjective: Describe the primary purpose of FAA Form 8130-3 (Authorized Release Certificate / Airworthiness Approval Tag)
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that FAA Form 8130-3 is an Authorized Release Certificate (also called the Airworthiness Approval Tag) used to approve new or overhauled aircraft parts for return to service, providing traceability and airworthiness attestation that the part meets approved design data.

flashcard.definition: FAA Form 8130-3 is the Authorized Release Certificate (commonly called the Airworthiness Approval Tag). It certifies that new or overhauled aircraft parts conform to approved design data and are in a condition for safe operation. It provides traceability and is used to transfer airworthiness attestation when parts move between owners or installers.
flashcard.analogy: An 8130-3 tag is like a certificate of authenticity for an aircraft part — it documents that the part has been approved for return to service.

jeopardy.question: This is the primary purpose of FAA Form 8130-3 (Authorized Release Certificate / Airworthiness Approval Tag).
choices: To approve new or overhauled aircraft parts for return to service and provide traceability that the part meets approved design data
To record a pilot's flight review
To document an aircraft's registration status
To serve as a sales invoice for aircraft parts
jeopardy.explanation: The 8130-3 is an airworthiness-approval and traceability document for aircraft parts. It is not related to pilot flight reviews, aircraft registration, or sales invoicing (though the tag often accompanies sales of new or overhauled parts).

multipleChoice.question: What is the primary purpose of FAA Form 8130-3 (Airworthiness Approval Tag)?
choices: To certify that new or overhauled aircraft parts conform
To record a pilot's flight review completion
To document an aircraft's registration
To act as a sales invoice for aircraft parts
multipleChoice.explanation: Form 8130-3 certifies that aircraft parts meet approved design data and are airworthy. The tag provides traceability that travels with the part. It is not a flight-review, registration, or sales document — though it commonly accompanies sales of new or overhauled parts.

practice.question: A technician receives an avionics unit with an FAA Form 8130-3 tag attached. What does that tag tell the technician?
choices: The unit conforms to approved design data and has been approved for return to service per the issuer's statement, providing airworthiness traceability for the part
The unit was sold recently and is still under warranty
The aircraft the unit is installed in is registered with the FAA
The pilot who flew the aircraft is current on their flight review
practice.explanation: The 8130-3 certifies conformance to approved design data and approval for return to service. It gives the installer confidence that the part is airworthy and provides a traceable chain from approval to installation. Warranty, aircraft registration, and pilot currency are separate matters.

preassessment.question: What is FAA Form 8130-3 used for?
choices: Certifying aircraft parts
Pilot flight reviews
Aircraft registration
Parts sales invoicing

questions.learningObjective: Identify the FAA's definition of maintenance
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that per 14 CFR Part 1, 'maintenance' means inspection, overhaul, repair, preservation, and the replacement of parts — but excludes preventive maintenance, which is separately defined.

flashcard.definition: 14 CFR Part 1 defines 'maintenance' as inspection, overhaul, repair, preservation, and the replacement of parts, BUT excludes preventive maintenance. Preventive maintenance is separately defined as the simple or minor preservation operations listed in Part 43 Appendix A.
flashcard.analogy: 'Maintenance' in the FAA sense is a broad category that INCLUDES many things but specifically EXCLUDES the narrow list of preventive-maintenance tasks.

jeopardy.question: According to FAA regulations, this is the definition of 'maintenance.'
choices: Any activity that keeps an aircraft operating
Inspection, overhaul, repair, preservation, and the replacement of parts, but excluding preventive maintenance
Only replacing failed parts
Any work performed by an A&P mechanic
jeopardy.explanation: The Part 1 definition is specific: maintenance = inspection, overhaul, repair, preservation, and parts replacement — but NOT preventive maintenance, which is defined separately. General phrases like 'any activity' or 'any work by an A&P' are not the regulatory definition.

multipleChoice.question: According to FAA regulations, what is the definition of 'maintenance'?
choices: Any activity that keeps an aircraft operating
Inspection, overhaul, repair, preservation, and parts replacement
Only the replacement of failed parts
Any work performed by a certificated A&P mechanic
multipleChoice.explanation: 14 CFR Part 1 defines maintenance as inspection, overhaul, repair, preservation, and parts replacement, explicitly excluding preventive maintenance. Preventive maintenance is a separate category limited to the simple operations listed in Part 43 Appendix A.

practice.question: A student asks where the line is between 'maintenance' and 'preventive maintenance' in FAA regulations. What is the best answer?
choices: Both are the same — the terms are interchangeable

Maintenance (per Part 1) is a broad category: inspection, overhaul, repair, preservation, and parts replacement. Preventive maintenance is a separate narrower category consisting only of the simple or minor preservation operations listed in Part 43 Appendix A
Maintenance refers only to major repairs; everything else is preventive
The distinction is made by individual technician judgment
practice.explanation: The FAA draws a clear regulatory distinction: maintenance is the broader category; preventive maintenance is a narrower category limited to the specific items in Appendix A. The distinction matters because preventive maintenance can be performed by certain pilot-owners, while general maintenance requires appropriately certificated mechanics or repair stations.

preassessment.question: What is the FAA definition of 'maintenance'?
choices: Any activity keeping an aircraft operating
Inspection, overhaul, repair, preservation, and parts
Only replacing failed parts
Any A&P-performed work

questions.learningObjective: Explain why Part 145 repair stations must keep work areas and walkways clear of clutter
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that Part 145 requires repair stations to maintain clean, organized work areas and clear walkways because clutter creates safety hazards (trip, fall, fire) and quality risks (FOD, tool loss, damaged parts).

flashcard.definition: Part 145 requires repair stations to maintain clean, organized work areas and clear walkways. Clutter creates both safety hazards (trip, fall, fire hazards from combustibles) and quality risks (FOD, lost tools, damaged parts, inability to find components). Housekeeping is a recurring Part 145 compliance item.
flashcard.analogy: A cluttered shop is dangerous and unreliable — both safety and workmanship suffer when basic organization lapses.

jeopardy.question: This is why a Part 145 repair station must keep walkways and work areas free of clutter.
choices: For visual appeal during FAA inspections only
Because Part 145 has no such requirement
To comply with OSHA but not Part 145
To reduce safety hazards (trip, fall, fire) and quality risks (FOD, lost tools, damaged parts)
jeopardy.explanation: Housekeeping addresses both safety and quality. Part 145 does include housekeeping-related requirements, and OSHA adds workplace safety requirements — the two are complementary, not alternatives. Visual appeal during inspections is a side effect, not the primary reason.

multipleChoice.question: In a Part 145 general-aviation repair station, why is it crucial to keep walkways and work areas free of clutter?
choices: For visual appeal during FAA audits only
Clutter is not actually a concern in Part 145 shops
Only OSHA requires clean walkways — Part 145 has no housekeeping rules
Because clutter creates real safety hazards
multipleChoice.explanation: Housekeeping in a Part 145 shop matters for safety (trips, falls, fire loads) and quality (FOD control, tool accountability, parts integrity). Part 145 includes housekeeping-related requirements, and OSHA layers additional safety requirements on top. Both apply simultaneously.

practice.question: A technician notices that the shop's main walkway has accumulated old parts boxes, cable offcuts, and a few spilled fluid-absorbent rags. Is this a Part 145 issue, and what is the appropriate response?
choices: No — as long as the boxes are not on the work benches
No — repair stations are not subject to housekeeping requirements
Yes — but only if the FAA inspector is scheduled to visit

Yes — it creates safety hazards (trip, fall, fire), quality risks (FOD, part loss), and is a Part 145 housekeeping issue. The immediate response is to clear the walkway, properly dispose of the rags (fluid-soaked rags can self-heat), and follow up with the responsible personnel to prevent recurrence
practice.explanation: Cluttered walkways are a Part 145 issue plus a safety concern (fluid-soaked rags are a fire risk). Proper response is immediate cleanup, proper disposal of fluid-contaminated materials, and follow-up to address the root cause. The rule applies continuously — not only when FAA inspectors are present.

preassessment.question: Why must Part 145 shops keep walkways clear?
choices: Visual appeal only
No such requirement exists
OSHA only, not Part 145
To reduce safety hazards and quality risks

questions.learningObjective: Describe pre-employment drug testing requirements under a Part 145 station's FAA-approved program
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that if a Part 145 station's FAA-approved antidrug program includes pre-employment drug testing, a technician must complete and pass that test prior to beginning work in a safety-sensitive position, in compliance with 14 CFR Part 120.

flashcard.definition: Under 14 CFR Part 120 and when incorporated into a Part 145 station's FAA-approved antidrug program, a prospective technician in a safety-sensitive position must complete and pass a pre-employment drug test before beginning work. The station's specific program details (testing substances, testing laboratory, consequences) are documented per Part 120 requirements.
flashcard.analogy: Pre-employment drug testing is a standard safety-sensitive hiring gate — complete and pass before starting the job.

jeopardy.question: If a Part 145 station's FAA-approved antidrug program includes pre-employment drug testing, this is required of a new technician.
choices: Complete and pass the pre-employment drug test before starting work in a safety-sensitive position
Ignore the test if they feel confident they would pass
Complete the test at any time during the first year of employment
Self-certify on an honor system
jeopardy.explanation: Pre-employment testing must be completed and passed BEFORE starting work in a safety-sensitive position. It cannot be deferred, ignored, or self-certified. Part 120 sets the federal requirements; Part 145 stations incorporate them into their programs.

multipleChoice.question: If a pre-employment drug test is part of a Part 145 station's FAA-approved antidrug program, what must the technician do?
choices: Complete and pass the pre-employment drug test before starting work
Ignore the test if they are confident they would pass
Complete the test at any point within the first year of employment
Self-certify on an honor system that they are drug-free
multipleChoice.explanation: Pre-employment testing must be completed and passed before starting safety-sensitive duties. Deferral, avoidance, and self-certification are not acceptable under Part 120 or the station's program. This is a non-negotiable hiring gate for Part 145 technicians in safety-sensitive roles.

practice.question: A new technician has accepted a position at a Part 145 repair station whose FAA-approved antidrug program includes pre-employment testing. What is the correct sequence of events before the technician can begin work?
choices: The technician must complete and pass the pre-employment drug test before beginning work in a safety-sensitive position. Only after the test is complete and the result is negative may the technician begin safety-sensitive duties
Start work immediately; the test can be completed at any time
Begin work if the technician self-certifies they are drug-free
Complete the test within a year of starting work
practice.explanation: Pre-employment testing is a gating requirement. The sequence is: conditional offer, test, negative result, then start of safety-sensitive work. Starting work before the test is complete, or relying on self-certification, violates Part 120 and the station's approved program.

preassessment.question: If pre-employment drug testing is part of a Part 145 program, when must the technician complete it?
choices: Before beginning
Anytime within year 1
Only if they wish
Via self-certification

questions.learningObjective: Describe the calibration verification required before using specialized test equipment in a Part 145 shop
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that before using specialized test equipment in a Part 145 shop, a technician must confirm the equipment's calibration is current and traceable, per the station's approved procedures and the equipment manufacturer's calibration interval.

flashcard.definition: Before using specialized test equipment in a Part 145 shop, the technician must confirm the equipment's calibration is current and traceable to a recognized standard. The station's procedures manual and the equipment manufacturer's interval govern the required calibration cycle. Out-of-calibration equipment cannot produce valid test results and cannot be used for maintenance decisions.
flashcard.analogy: Using out-of-cal test equipment is like using a bent ruler — the measurement looks plausible but is not valid.

jeopardy.question: Before using any specialized test equipment in a Part 145 environment, this must be confirmed.
choices: The technician has used the equipment before
The equipment is the latest model
The equipment was purchased within the last year
The equipment's calibration is current and traceable per the station's procedures and the manufacturer's interval
jeopardy.explanation: Calibration currency and traceability are the regulatory requirements. Familiarity, model age, or recent purchase do not substitute for verified calibration. Out-of-calibration equipment cannot be used for maintenance decisions.

multipleChoice.question: Before using any specialized test equipment in a Part 145 environment, a technician must confirm:
choices: That the technician has previously used this specific piece of equipment
That the equipment is the newest model available
That the equipment was purchased within the last year
That the equipment's calibration is current and traceable
multipleChoice.explanation: Calibration currency and traceability are required. The station's procedures manual specifies calibration intervals consistent with the manufacturer's recommendations. Out-of-calibration equipment cannot produce valid results and must not be used for maintenance decisions regardless of how familiar or new it is.

practice.question: A technician picks up a digital multimeter from the shop's shared tool area. The calibration sticker on the meter shows the last calibration was 14 months ago, and the sticker indicates the interval is 12 months. Can the technician use this meter?
choices: Yes — 14 months is close enough to 12 months
Yes — if the technician personally verifies the meter against a battery voltage
Yes — if the technician documents the discrepancy in the work order

No — the meter is out of its calibration interval and cannot be used for valid measurements in a Part 145 environment. The meter must be sent for calibration before use, and the technician should select a different, in-calibration meter in the meantime
practice.explanation: An instrument past its calibration date cannot produce valid measurements in a Part 145 environment, regardless of how small the overage. The meter must be recalibrated before use. Battery-voltage spot-checks and documentation of the discrepancy do not substitute for formal calibration.

preassessment.question: Before using specialized test equipment in a Part 145 shop, confirm:
choices: Prior familiarity with the equipment
The equipment is the newest model
It was recently purchased
Calibration is current and traceable

questions.learningObjective: Identify the appropriate first troubleshooting step when a pilot reports intermittent VOR NAV flag indications
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that the appropriate first troubleshooting step for intermittent VOR NAV flag indications in a Part 145 shop is to verify the basic installation — antenna, coax, connectors, and power — before replacing the receiver or other internal components.

flashcard.term: VOR NAV flag troubleshooting — first step
flashcard.definition: For intermittent VOR NAV flag indications, the first step in a Part 145 shop is to verify physical-layer items: antenna condition and secure mounting, coax integrity and connector tightness, and adequate power to the receiver. Most intermittent NAV flag issues trace to installation or wiring problems rather than receiver internal faults.
flashcard.analogy: Like any intermittent signal problem, start with the physical path — most causes are there, not in the electronics.

jeopardy.question: For intermittent VOR NAV flag indications, this is a useful first step a Part 145 technician can take.
choices: Immediately replace the VOR receiver
Only write up the squawk and return the aircraft to service
Rewrite the aircraft logbook
Verify physical-layer items first: antenna condition and mounting, coax integrity and connector tightness, and adequate power to the receiver
jeopardy.explanation: Physical-layer checks are the first step for intermittent symptoms. Receiver replacement is expensive and often unnecessary. A write-up with no action is not a resolution. Logbook rewriting is not appropriate.

multipleChoice.question: A pilot reports intermittent NAV flag indications on a VOR receiver. Which step might a new technician take FIRST in a Part 145 shop?
choices: Immediately replace the VOR receiver
Write up the squawk and return the aircraft to service without action
Rewrite the aircraft's logbook
Verify the physical installation first — check antenna condition and mounting
multipleChoice.explanation: Intermittent symptoms typically originate in the physical layer — antennas, coax, connectors, and power feeds. These checks are cheap and often resolve the issue. Only after physical-layer items are verified should receiver internals be investigated. Replacing the receiver first is expensive and often unnecessary.

practice.question: A technician is troubleshooting intermittent NAV flag indications on a VOR. Which sequence of first-steps is most appropriate in a Part 145 shop?
choices: Replace the receiver and flight-test
Send the aircraft home and tell the pilot to fly it and see if the problem returns
Swap the NAV head unit with a neighbor aircraft

Inspect the VOR antenna for physical damage and secure mounting; verify coax continuity and shield integrity; check all connector pins for tightness and corrosion; verify adequate and stable power to the receiver. Only after these physical-layer items are confirmed good should internal receiver faults be investigated
practice.explanation: The correct first-step sequence is a systematic physical-layer check. This is consistent with best Part 145 troubleshooting practice — start with cheap, low-risk checks that resolve most intermittent symptoms before moving to expensive replacement. Ad-hoc swaps, sending the aircraft home without action, and immediate receiver replacement are all poor practice.

preassessment.question: What is a good first step for intermittent VOR NAV flag indications?
choices: Replace the receiver
Return the aircraft to service untouched
Rewrite the logbook
Verify antenna, coax, connectors, and power

questions.learningObjective: Predict airspeed indicator behavior when the pitot tube becomes blocked during a climb
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that a blocked pitot tube causes the airspeed indicator to behave like an altimeter: during a climb, indicated airspeed increases (because static pressure decreases while ram pressure in the blocked system remains trapped), falsely suggesting an increase in speed.

flashcard.term: Blocked pitot, aircraft climbing — ASI response
flashcard.definition: If the pitot tube becomes blocked (ram-air inlet) while the aircraft climbs, the ASI behaves like an altimeter: indicated airspeed INCREASES. The trapped ram-side pressure stays constant while the static-side pressure decreases with altitude, so the differential grows and the ASI reads higher even though true airspeed has not changed.
flashcard.analogy: A blocked pitot acts like a sealed bottle — its internal pressure stays the same while outside pressure drops, so the differential grows and the ASI reads faster.

jeopardy.question: If the pitot tube becomes blocked while the aircraft climbs, this is what happens to the indicated airspeed.
choices: It increases
It decreases toward zero
It remains unchanged
It oscillates randomly
jeopardy.explanation: A blocked pitot traps ram pressure. As the aircraft climbs, static pressure drops, but trapped ram pressure does not — the ASI differential grows and reads higher. The ASI effectively behaves like an altimeter.

multipleChoice.question: If the pitot tube becomes blocked while the aircraft climbs, how does the indicated airspeed respond?
choices: It increases
It decreases toward zero
It remains unchanged regardless of altitude
It oscillates randomly with no pattern
multipleChoice.explanation: A blocked pitot acts like a sealed pressure reservoir. During climb, static pressure drops but trapped ram pressure does not, so the measured differential (which the ASI displays as airspeed) grows. The ASI reads higher even though true airspeed has not changed — this is a dangerous failure that can cause pilots to unknowingly slow to stall speed while watching a 'normal' airspeed reading.

practice.question: A pilot reports that during a climb the airspeed indicator continued to read an abnormally high airspeed even as the aircraft slowed and approached a stall. The static system appears normal. What is the most likely cause?
choices: The pitot tube is blocked — trapped ram pressure caused the ASI to read higher as static pressure decreased with altitude, a textbook blocked-pitot climb signature
The airspeed indicator is miscalibrated
The aircraft was overweight
The engine was developing less power than expected
practice.explanation: This is the classic blocked-pitot-in-climb signature. The ASI behaves like an altimeter when the pitot inlet is blocked — reading higher as the aircraft climbs, regardless of true airspeed. The fix is to clear the pitot obstruction (often ice, in which case pitot heat should have prevented the blockage).

preassessment.question: Blocked pitot during climb — what happens to indicated airspeed?
choices: Increases
Decreases
Unchanged
Oscillates

questions.learningObjective: Predict airspeed indicator behavior when the pitot tube becomes blocked during a descent
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that a blocked pitot tube during a descent causes the indicated airspeed to DECREASE as static pressure increases, because the trapped ram pressure stays constant while static pressure rises, reducing the differential and reading lower airspeed.

flashcard.term: Blocked pitot, aircraft descending — ASI response
flashcard.definition: If the pitot tube is blocked during descent, the ASI reads LOWER. The trapped ram pressure stays constant while the static pressure increases during descent, reducing the differential and decreasing the indicated airspeed — regardless of true airspeed.
flashcard.analogy: During descent, the outside pressure rises while the sealed pitot pressure stays the same — the differential shrinks and the ASI reads slower.

jeopardy.question: If the pitot tube is blocked while the aircraft descends, this is how the airspeed indicator responds.
choices: It reads higher than actual airspeed
It oscillates wildly
It reads lower than actual airspeed, because trapped ram pressure stays constant while static pressure increases during descent
It remains unchanged
jeopardy.explanation: A blocked pitot during descent: trapped ram pressure is constant, static pressure rises, differential shrinks, ASI reads lower. This is the opposite behavior from the climb case.

multipleChoice.question: If the pitot tube is blocked while the aircraft descends, how does the airspeed indicator respond?
choices: It reads higher than actual airspeed
It oscillates wildly between readings
It reads lower than actual airspeed
It remains unchanged regardless of altitude
multipleChoice.explanation: During descent with a blocked pitot, static pressure increases but trapped ram pressure stays the same, so the differential shrinks and the ASI reads lower. This can be equally dangerous — pilots may speed up trying to maintain a 'normal' airspeed reading while actually exceeding Vne.

practice.question: A pilot in descent sees the airspeed indicator reading lower than expected, even as the aircraft feels faster and the engine sounds higher. The static system checks normal. What is most likely happening?
choices: The engine is not producing enough power
The airspeed indicator has failed mechanically
The pitot tube is blocked — trapped ram pressure combined with increasing static pressure during descent causes the ASI to read lower, possibly masking dangerous overspeed conditions
The pilot is over-reading the instrument
practice.explanation: This pattern — descent + lower-than-expected ASI + aircraft feels fast — is the blocked-pitot-in-descent signature. The masking of true airspeed is especially dangerous because the pilot may push speed higher to 'match' the low reading, potentially exceeding Vne.

preassessment.question: Blocked pitot during descent — ASI reads:
choices: Higher than actual
Wildly oscillating
Lower than actual
Unchanged

questions.learningObjective: Predict altimeter and VSI behavior when the static port is blocked
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that a blocked static port causes the altimeter to freeze at the altitude the port was blocked at and the VSI to read zero (no rate of change), regardless of the aircraft's actual altitude changes.

flashcard.term: Blocked static port — altimeter and VSI
flashcard.definition: If the static port is blocked, the altimeter freezes at the altitude at which the blockage occurred, and the VSI reads zero regardless of actual climb or descent rate. Both instruments depend on changing static pressure to indicate altitude and rate of change; with the port sealed, no changes reach them.
flashcard.analogy: A blocked static port is like covering a barometer with a jar — the readings stop responding to the outside world.

jeopardy.question: If the static port is blocked, this is how it affects the altimeter and VSI.
choices: Altimeter reads erratically; VSI shows maximum climb rate
Altimeter freezes at the blockage altitude; VSI reads zero regardless of actual altitude changes
Both instruments read the correct altitude and rate
Altimeter reads zero; VSI reads full scale climb
jeopardy.explanation: Blocked static: altimeter freezes (no changing static pressure to respond to), VSI reads zero (no rate of change detectable). Both instruments appear to indicate that the aircraft is in perfectly level flight at a fixed altitude, regardless of actual behavior.

multipleChoice.question: If the static port is blocked, how does it affect the altimeter and the vertical speed indicator (VSI)?
choices: The altimeter reads erratically and the VSI shows a maximum climb rate
The altimeter freezes at the altitude at which the blockage occurred
Both instruments continue to read correctly because the pitot tube compensates
The altimeter reads zero and the VSI reads full-scale climb
multipleChoice.explanation: A blocked static port cuts off the changing-pressure signal that both instruments need. Altimeter freezes. VSI reads zero. This creates a dangerous illusion of level flight at a fixed altitude — the pilot's alternate static source (if equipped) is the immediate remedy.

practice.question: During flight, a pilot notices the altimeter is stuck at 5,500 feet and the VSI reads 0 feet per minute, even though the aircraft is clearly climbing (engine at climb power, attitude indicator showing nose-up). The airspeed indicator also reads lower than expected. What is the most likely cause?
choices: The altimeter and VSI have failed simultaneously — replace both

The static port is blocked — altimeter freezes at the blockage altitude, VSI reads zero, and the airspeed indicator also reads incorrectly (lower than actual in a climb) because ASI depends on the static reference. The immediate remedy is to open the alternate static source if equipped
The aircraft's altimeter setting is wrong
The pitot tube has iced up
practice.explanation: The combination of frozen altimeter + zero VSI + lower-than-expected ASI is the classic blocked-static signature. All three instruments share the static reference; blocking the port affects all three. The alternate static source (typically inside the cabin, lower pressure than outside) is the immediate remedy until the static port can be cleared on the ground.

preassessment.question: Blocked static port — altimeter and VSI behavior:
choices: Altimeter erratic, VSI max climb
Altimeter freezes; VSI reads zero
Both read correctly
Altimeter zero; VSI full climb

questions.learningObjective: Identify the flight instruments that rely on the static port
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that three flight instruments rely on static pressure for accurate readings: the altimeter, the vertical speed indicator (VSI), and the airspeed indicator (ASI — which uses static as its reference for pitot differential).

flashcard.definition: Three instruments rely on static pressure: the altimeter (senses static pressure directly to indicate altitude), the VSI (senses rate of change of static pressure to indicate climb/descent rate), and the airspeed indicator (uses static pressure as the reference against which ram pressure is compared). A blocked or leaking static port affects all three.
flashcard.analogy: The static port is the 'atmospheric reference' for three instruments. Lose the reference, and all three go wrong together.

jeopardy.question: These are the flight instruments that rely on the static port for accurate readings.
choices: Altimeter only
Attitude indicator, heading indicator, and turn coordinator
Altimeter, vertical speed indicator, and airspeed indicator
Only the vertical speed indicator
jeopardy.explanation: Three pitot-static instruments: altimeter, VSI, and ASI — all use static pressure. Gyro-driven instruments (attitude, heading, turn coordinator) do not use static pressure.

multipleChoice.question: Which flight instruments rely on the static port for accurate readings?
choices: Altimeter only
The attitude indicator, the heading indicator, and the turn coordinator
The altimeter, the vertical speed indicator, and the airspeed indicator
Only the vertical speed indicator
multipleChoice.explanation: The altimeter, VSI, and ASI all use static pressure. A static port problem affects all three simultaneously. Gyroscopic instruments (attitude, heading, turn coordinator) are independent of the static system.

practice.question: A technician is testing the aircraft pitot-static system. The plan calls for applying a controlled vacuum to the static port and watching for correct response on the pitot-static-driven instruments. Which three instruments should the technician expect to respond to the static input?
choices: Attitude indicator, heading indicator, and turn coordinator
Only the altimeter
Altimeter, vertical speed indicator, and airspeed indicator — these are the three instruments that rely on static pressure
Engine gauges and fuel gauges
practice.explanation: Altimeter, VSI, and ASI all respond to static pressure changes. A functional test of the static system verifies that all three respond correctly to controlled input. The gyro and engine instruments are unrelated.

preassessment.question: Which instruments rely on the static port?
choices: Altimeter only
Gyros
Altimeter, VSI
VSI only

questions.learningObjective: Describe the primary function of the pitot tube
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that the pitot tube's primary function is to sense ram air pressure (the total pressure created by the aircraft's forward motion through the air), which the airspeed indicator compares to static pressure to determine airspeed.

flashcard.definition: The pitot tube senses ram air pressure — the total pressure created by the aircraft moving through the air. The airspeed indicator compares this ram pressure to static pressure and displays the differential as indicated airspeed. The pitot tube is typically forward-facing and heated to prevent ice blockage.
flashcard.analogy: The pitot tube is the 'forward-facing scoop' that measures how hard the wind is pushing into it — more speed, more pressure, higher ASI reading.

jeopardy.question: This is the primary function of the pitot tube.
choices: To measure static atmospheric pressure
To sense ram air pressure for the airspeed indicator
To measure ambient temperature
To detect icing conditions
jeopardy.explanation: The pitot tube senses ram air (total) pressure, which the ASI uses. Static pressure is measured by the separate static port. Temperature and ice detection use different sensors.

multipleChoice.question: What is the primary function of the pitot tube on an aircraft?
choices: To measure static atmospheric pressure
To sense ram air pressure created by the aircraft's
To measure ambient outside air temperature
To detect icing conditions directly
multipleChoice.explanation: The pitot tube's job is to measure ram (total) pressure. The ASI takes the difference between ram and static pressures and displays it as airspeed. Static pressure is measured separately by the static port. Temperature and icing detection use other sensors.

practice.question: A technician is explaining the pitot tube to a student. Which is the simplest correct description?
choices: The pitot tube measures static atmospheric pressure

The pitot tube is a forward-facing probe that senses the ram air pressure created by the aircraft's forward motion; the airspeed indicator compares this ram pressure to static pressure to compute indicated airspeed
The pitot tube is a temperature probe
The pitot tube detects icing conditions
practice.explanation: The pitot tube senses ram pressure; the ASI takes the pitot-static differential and displays airspeed. The probe is typically heated to prevent ice, but ice detection itself is a separate system. Static pressure comes from the static port, not the pitot tube.

preassessment.question: What does the pitot tube do?
choices: Measure static pressure
Sense ram air pressure for the airspeed indicator
Measure temperature
Detect icing

questions.learningObjective: Describe what the static pressure system senses
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that the static pressure system senses the ambient (undisturbed) atmospheric pressure around the aircraft, which decreases with altitude and is used by the altimeter, VSI, and airspeed indicator as a reference.

flashcard.definition: The static pressure system senses the ambient atmospheric pressure — the undisturbed outside-air pressure that decreases with altitude. Static ports are located on the fuselage in positions where airflow is undisturbed by the aircraft's motion. Static pressure is used by the altimeter, VSI, and airspeed indicator.
flashcard.analogy: The static port measures the 'atmospheric pressure outside the airplane,' which is why all three instruments need it: altitude, rate of altitude change, and airspeed all reference it.

jeopardy.question: The static pressure system in an aircraft senses this.
choices: The aircraft's forward-motion ram air pressure
The temperature of the air
The ambient (undisturbed) atmospheric pressure around the aircraft, which decreases with altitude
The moisture content of the air
jeopardy.explanation: The static system measures undisturbed ambient atmospheric pressure. Ram pressure comes from the pitot tube. Temperature and moisture use different sensors.

multipleChoice.question: What does the static pressure system in an aircraft sense?
choices: The aircraft's forward-motion ram air pressure
The outside air temperature
The ambient atmospheric pressure around the aircraft
The moisture content of the air
multipleChoice.explanation: The static system senses ambient atmospheric pressure — unrelated to the aircraft's motion. Static ports are placed at locations on the fuselage where airflow is 'still' relative to the aircraft. Ram pressure (forward motion), temperature, and moisture are separate measurements.

practice.question: A technician verifying a pitot-static system calibration notes that the static port pressure changes with altitude but not with airspeed. Is this expected behavior?
choices: No — static pressure should vary with airspeed
No — static pressure should increase with airspeed
Yes — the static system senses ambient atmospheric pressure, which depends on altitude but not on the aircraft's speed. This is the defining characteristic of the static system
Yes, but only below 10,000 feet
practice.explanation: Static pressure is altitude-dependent and (ideally) airspeed-independent. Well-designed static ports are placed where airflow around the fuselage does not disturb the measurement. This is what distinguishes static pressure from ram pressure (pitot), which depends on airspeed.

preassessment.question: What does the static pressure system sense?
choices: Ram air pressure
Temperature
Ambient atmospheric
Humidity

questions.learningObjective: Identify likely causes of an airspeed indicator that consistently reads too low
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that an ASI consistently reading too low is typically caused by a partial pitot blockage (reduced ram pressure), a leak in the pitot-to-ASI plumbing, or a clogged/obstructed pitot drain that allows moisture to affect ram pressure.

flashcard.term: ASI consistently reads low — common causes
flashcard.definition: An ASI that consistently reads too low is typically caused by: (1) a partial pitot-tube blockage (ram inlet partly obstructed by dirt, insects, or ice residue), (2) a leak in the pitot-to-ASI plumbing, or (3) a clogged drain that allows moisture to affect the pitot line. The static side is less commonly a cause of consistently low readings.
flashcard.analogy: Low ASI readings = the pressure differential is smaller than it should be. Usually it's because less ram pressure is reaching the ASI.

jeopardy.question: This is a likely cause of an airspeed indicator that consistently reads too low.
choices: The airspeed indicator itself is over-calibrated
The aircraft is flying faster than rated
The altimeter is set incorrectly
A partial pitot blockage, a leak in the pitot-to-ASI plumbing, or a clogged pitot drain
jeopardy.explanation: Consistently low ASI readings typically trace to physical issues in the pitot side of the system: partial blockage, leaks, or drain problems. Instrument miscalibration is possible but less common. Altimeter settings do not affect the ASI.

multipleChoice.question: A pilot reports that the airspeed indicator consistently reads too low. Which is the most likely cause?
choices: The airspeed indicator itself is over-calibrated from the factory
The aircraft is flying faster than its rated speed
The altimeter setting is incorrect
A partial pitot blockage, a pitot-line leak, or a clogged drain
multipleChoice.explanation: Consistently low ASI readings point to reduced ram pressure at the ASI — caused by partial pitot blockage, pitot-line leaks, or drain issues. Instrument miscalibration is rare. Altimeter settings have no effect on the ASI; they affect only the altimeter's Kollsman reference.

practice.question: During a test flight after pitot-static maintenance, the pilot reports the ASI now reads consistently about 10 knots slower than GPS ground speed suggests. What is the most productive first check?
choices: Replace the airspeed indicator
Adjust the altimeter setting
Check the VSI calibration

Inspect the pitot tube for partial obstruction (dirt, insects, residue), check the pitot-to-ASI plumbing for leaks, and verify the pitot drain is clear — these are the most common causes of consistently-low ASI readings, and can be introduced by recent maintenance
practice.explanation: After maintenance work, newly low ASI readings are often traceable to something done during the work — a loose fitting, a missed O-ring, or trapped moisture. Inspect the physical pitot path first. Instrument replacement and altimeter/VSI adjustments are premature without evidence they are needed.

preassessment.question: ASI reads too low — likely cause?
choices: Over-calibration
Aircraft flying too fast
Wrong altimeter setting
Partial pitot blockage

questions.learningObjective: Identify the likely cause of a VSI that shows a constant rate of descent in level flight
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that a VSI showing constant descent in level flight is most likely caused by a slow leak in the static line to the VSI, a clogged VSI calibration orifice, or a miscalibrated instrument — rather than an actual aircraft problem.

flashcard.term: VSI shows constant descent in level flight — cause
flashcard.definition: A VSI that shows a constant rate of descent even in level flight is most likely caused by a slow leak in the VSI's static line (slow pressure equalization creates an apparent rate), a clogged VSI calibration orifice (which produces non-zero output at steady state), or instrument miscalibration. A small leak is the most common cause in the field.
flashcard.analogy: A constant false descent reading means the VSI is 'seeing' a slow pressure change that isn't actually there — usually a leak that never quite equalizes.

jeopardy.question: This is the most likely cause of a VSI showing a constant rate of descent even in level flight.
choices: The aircraft is actually descending without the pilot noticing
The altimeter is set incorrectly
The pitot tube is heated
A slow leak in the VSI's static line, a clogged VSI calibration orifice, or a miscalibrated instrument
jeopardy.explanation: Constant descent in level flight is almost always a VSI system problem. Slow static-line leak is the most common in the field. Altimeter setting does not affect the VSI. Pitot heat is unrelated.

multipleChoice.question: A vertical speed indicator (VSI) shows a constant rate of descent even in level flight. Which is the most likely cause?
choices: The aircraft is actually in a slow, constant descent that the pilot has not noticed
The altimeter is set to the wrong local pressure
The pitot tube has been heated
A slow leak in the VSI's static line, a clogged calibration orifice, or a miscalibrated instrument
multipleChoice.explanation: A constant false descent indication in level flight is a classic symptom of a VSI system issue — usually a slow static-line leak that never equalizes, or a clogged calibration orifice that produces non-zero output at steady state. The altimeter would confirm actual altitude; if the altimeter is stable the aircraft is in level flight, ruling out option A.

practice.question: A technician is verifying a VSI that reads a constant 100 fpm descent even when the aircraft is level and the altimeter is stable. What is the most productive first check?
choices: Replace the VSI immediately
Adjust the aircraft's weight and balance
Reset the transponder

Perform a static-system leak check — a small leak in the VSI's static line is the most common cause of a false constant descent reading. If the static system is tight, investigate the calibration orifice
practice.explanation: A static-system leak check is the standard first step. A small leak allows pressure to slowly equalize through the leak path instead of through the calibration orifice, producing a false rate reading. If the static system checks tight, the next step is bench checking the VSI's calibration orifice. Weight/balance and transponder resets are unrelated.

preassessment.question: VSI shows constant descent in level flight — likely cause?
choices: Aircraft actually descending
Wrong altimeter setting
Pitot heat on
Static line leak, clogged orifice

questions.learningObjective: Describe what an altimeter measures
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that an altimeter measures atmospheric pressure and converts it to altitude using a standard pressure-altitude relationship, allowing the pilot to fly at known altitudes relative to a reference (sea level or a set local pressure).

flashcard.definition: The altimeter measures static atmospheric pressure and converts it to altitude using a standard pressure-altitude relationship. The pilot sets a local altimeter setting (Kollsman window) so the indicated altitude is referenced appropriately (MSL above sea level). Atmospheric pressure decreases with altitude at a known rate (approximately 1 inch Hg per 1,000 feet at low altitudes).
flashcard.analogy: An altimeter is a pressure-sensing barometer calibrated to display the height that corresponds to the sensed pressure.

jeopardy.question: This is what an altimeter measures.
choices: Ram air pressure for airspeed
Atmospheric (static) pressure, which the instrument converts to altitude
Aircraft attitude
Aircraft heading
jeopardy.explanation: The altimeter measures static atmospheric pressure and displays it as altitude. Ram pressure is used by the ASI. Attitude and heading are gyroscopic/magnetic instrument measurements.

multipleChoice.question: What does an altimeter measure?
choices: Ram air pressure for the airspeed indicator
Atmospheric (static) pressure
Aircraft pitch and bank attitude
Aircraft heading relative to magnetic north
multipleChoice.explanation: An altimeter senses static atmospheric pressure and translates it into altitude. Lower pressure means higher altitude. Ram pressure, attitude, and heading are measured by other instruments with different operating principles.

practice.question: A student asks how an altimeter 'knows' how high the aircraft is. What is the best explanation?
choices: GPS tells the altimeter the altitude

The altimeter measures static atmospheric pressure, which decreases predictably with altitude at a known rate (about 1 inch Hg per 1,000 feet near sea level). The instrument converts that pressure to altitude using a standard scale, with the local altimeter setting adjusting the reference to account for current weather
The altimeter measures radar altitude above terrain
The altimeter uses an inertial navigation system
practice.explanation: The classic altimeter is a pressure instrument. Radar altimeters are separate devices that measure height above terrain directly. GPS altitude is independent. Inertial systems integrate acceleration to compute position but are not part of a pressure altimeter's operation.

preassessment.question: What does an altimeter measure?
choices: Ram air pressure
Atmospheric static
Attitude
Heading

questions.learningObjective: Describe how a pilot sets the altimeter before flight
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that before flight the pilot sets the altimeter by rotating the Kollsman knob to match the current local altimeter setting (usually reported by ATIS, AWOS, or ATC) so that the altimeter indicates field elevation when the aircraft is on the ground.

flashcard.definition: Before flight the pilot sets the altimeter's Kollsman window to the current local altimeter setting — a barometric pressure reported in inches of mercury (inHg) or hectopascals (hPa). When set correctly, the altimeter reads field elevation when the aircraft is on the ground. Current settings are available from ATIS, AWOS, or ATC.
flashcard.analogy: Setting the altimeter is like calibrating a scale to a known weight before using it — you need a reference to make the reading accurate.

jeopardy.question: Before flight, this is how the altimeter is set.
choices: To the current local altimeter setting (from ATIS, AWOS, or ATC) so the altimeter reads field elevation on the ground
To zero, regardless of altitude
To standard atmosphere (29.92 inHg) for all flights
To whatever reading the previous pilot left it at
jeopardy.explanation: The altimeter is set to the current local altimeter setting before flight. This produces a reading of field elevation on the ground. Standard (29.92) is used only at and above FL180 in US airspace (transition altitude). Zeroing or using a previous setting are both incorrect.

multipleChoice.question: Before flight, a pilot sets the altimeter to:
choices: The current local altimeter setting (obtained from ATIS
Zero, regardless of actual altitude
Standard atmosphere (29.92 inHg) for every flight
Whatever the previous pilot left it at
multipleChoice.explanation: The pre-flight altimeter setting is the current local barometric pressure, which makes the altimeter read field elevation on the ground. ATIS, AWOS, and ATC provide this. Standard (29.92) is used only at and above the transition altitude (FL180 in the US). Zeroing and keeping prior settings are both incorrect.

practice.question: A pilot tunes the local ATIS and hears 'altimeter 30.07.' Before flight, how should the pilot set the altimeter?
choices: Set the Kollsman window to 30.07 inHg; when correctly set, the altimeter should indicate field elevation while the aircraft is on the ground. Verify this against the published field elevation as a cross-check
Set it to 29.92 for standard atmosphere
Leave it at whatever the previous pilot set
Set it to zero
practice.explanation: The pilot sets the Kollsman window to the reported value (30.07 inHg) and cross-checks against the published field elevation. A large discrepancy between indicated altitude and field elevation points to an altimeter problem that must be investigated before flight. Standard setting (29.92) is used above the transition altitude, not on the ground.

preassessment.question: Before flight, the altimeter is set to:
choices: Current local altimeter setting
Zero
29.92 standard
Whatever the last pilot set

questions.learningObjective: Describe the primary purpose of the vertical speed indicator (VSI)
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that the vertical speed indicator displays the aircraft's rate of climb or descent (in feet per minute), derived from the rate of change of static pressure.

flashcard.definition: The vertical speed indicator (VSI) displays the aircraft's rate of climb or descent in feet per minute (fpm), derived by sensing the rate of change of static atmospheric pressure. Positive readings indicate climb; negative readings indicate descent; zero indicates level flight.
flashcard.analogy: The VSI is a 'first derivative' of altitude — it shows how fast the altitude is changing, not the altitude itself.

jeopardy.question: This is the primary purpose of the vertical speed indicator (VSI).
choices: To display the aircraft's current altitude
To display the aircraft's airspeed
To display the aircraft's heading
To display the aircraft's rate of climb or descent in feet per minute
jeopardy.explanation: The VSI shows rate of altitude change, not absolute altitude (that's the altimeter), airspeed (ASI), or heading (heading indicator).

multipleChoice.question: What is the primary purpose of the vertical speed indicator (VSI)?
choices: To display the aircraft's current altitude
To display the aircraft's airspeed
To display the aircraft's heading relative to magnetic north
To display the aircraft's rate of climb or descent
multipleChoice.explanation: The VSI shows the rate at which altitude is changing. Zero reading = level flight. Positive = climbing; negative = descending. It is the 'first derivative' of the altimeter's altitude display.

practice.question: A pilot is flying a constant-rate descent of 500 feet per minute to reach the initial approach fix at the correct altitude. Which instrument most directly helps the pilot maintain this rate?
choices: The altimeter, which shows current altitude
The airspeed indicator
The heading indicator
The VSI, which directly displays the rate of descent in feet per minute — the pilot maintains the desired rate by keeping the VSI needle at 500 fpm down
practice.explanation: The VSI directly displays rate, which is exactly the parameter being controlled. The altimeter shows current altitude but not rate. Airspeed and heading are separate parameters. Pilots manage descent profiles primarily by VSI reference with altimeter cross-checks.

preassessment.question: What does the VSI show?
choices: Current altitude
Airspeed
Heading
Rate of climb or descent

questions.learningObjective: Identify a flight instrument commonly powered by the aircraft's vacuum system
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that the attitude indicator (and heading indicator, in some installations) in traditional general-aviation aircraft is driven by a gyroscope powered by the engine-driven vacuum pump, using suction to spin the gyro at high RPM.

flashcard.definition: In traditional general-aviation aircraft, the attitude indicator — and often the heading indicator (directional gyro) — is driven by a vacuum-powered gyroscope. An engine-driven vacuum pump provides suction that spins the internal gyro at high RPM, maintaining its stable reference. Turn coordinators are more commonly electric.
flashcard.analogy: Vacuum pumps suck air through the gyro housing, making the rotor spin like a pinwheel — fast enough to maintain rigidity in space.

jeopardy.question: This flight instrument is commonly powered by the aircraft's vacuum system in traditional GA aircraft.
choices: Airspeed indicator
Altimeter
Magnetic compass
Attitude indicator
jeopardy.explanation: The attitude indicator (and often the heading indicator) in traditional GA is vacuum-driven. The ASI and altimeter are pressure instruments. The magnetic compass is passive — no power needed.

multipleChoice.question: Which flight instrument is commonly powered by the aircraft's vacuum system in a traditional general-aviation aircraft?
choices: Airspeed indicator
Altimeter
Magnetic compass
Attitude indicator
multipleChoice.explanation: Traditional attitude indicators and heading indicators are vacuum-driven gyroscopes. The vacuum pump provides suction that spins the internal gyros. Airspeed indicator and altimeter are pressure-based (pitot-static) instruments. The magnetic compass is a passive instrument that uses Earth's magnetic field.

practice.question: A pilot reports that the attitude indicator is slowly becoming unreliable while the altimeter and airspeed indicator continue working correctly. Which component is most likely to be the problem?
choices: The pitot-static system
The magnetic compass
The GPS receiver
The engine-driven vacuum pump (or associated plumbing and vacuum regulator) — the attitude indicator is vacuum-driven, while the altimeter and ASI are independent pitot-static instruments
practice.explanation: An attitude indicator problem with healthy pitot-static instruments points to the vacuum system. The vacuum pump is a wear item and commonly fails; a failing pump leads to gradual gyro degradation. Pitot-static, compass, and GPS are independent systems.

preassessment.question: Which instrument uses the aircraft vacuum system?
choices: ASI
Altimeter
Magnetic compass
Attitude indicator

questions.learningObjective: Identify the gyroscopic principle that allows a gyroscopic instrument to maintain a stable reference
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that rigidity in space is the gyroscopic principle that allows a gyroscope to maintain a stable spatial reference — once spinning, the gyro resists changes to its axis orientation unless a force is applied.

flashcard.definition: Rigidity in space is the gyroscopic principle that allows a spinning gyroscope to maintain a stable spatial reference. The spinning mass resists changes to the orientation of its spin axis — unless an external force causes precession. This stability is what allows gyros to provide attitude, heading, and turn references even as the aircraft maneuvers around them.
flashcard.analogy: A spinning top resists being tipped over — the same physics keeps gyro instruments stable while the aircraft pitches, rolls, and yaws around them.

jeopardy.question: This gyroscopic principle allows gyroscopic instruments to maintain a stable reference.
choices: Rigidity in space
Precession
Angular momentum only when accelerating
Brownian motion
jeopardy.explanation: Rigidity in space is the correct term. Precession is a related but different phenomenon (the response of a spinning gyro to an applied force). 'Angular momentum only when accelerating' is not a valid physical concept. Brownian motion is unrelated (random thermal motion).

multipleChoice.question: Which gyroscopic principle allows gyroscopic instruments to maintain a stable reference regardless of aircraft maneuvering?
choices: Rigidity in space
Precession
Angular momentum only when accelerating
Brownian motion
multipleChoice.explanation: Rigidity in space is the key principle for gyroscopic stability. Once spinning, the gyro resists changes to its axis orientation. Precession is a different but related concept — it describes how a spinning gyroscope responds to an applied force (the response appears 90 degrees around the rotation from where the force was applied). Both principles matter, but stability comes from rigidity.

practice.question: A student pilot asks how an attitude indicator 'knows' which way is up, even during aggressive maneuvering. What is the correct explanation?
choices: The instrument uses a spinning gyroscope whose axis of rotation maintains a stable spatial reference (rigidity in space). The aircraft structure pitches and rolls around the gyro while the gyro itself remains oriented to its original axis, giving the pilot a visual reference for attitude
The instrument uses an internal accelerometer array
The instrument uses GPS directly
The instrument measures atmospheric pressure in multiple directions
practice.explanation: The traditional attitude indicator uses a gyroscope exhibiting rigidity in space. The gyro is initially aligned with Earth's local vertical, and its own inertia keeps it there while the aircraft maneuvers around it. Modern AHRS replaces the mechanical gyro with solid-state sensors but operates on analogous principles.

preassessment.question: What principle makes gyroscopes maintain a stable reference?
choices: Rigidity in space
Precession
Angular momentum only while accelerating
Brownian motion

questions.learningObjective: Identify the flight instruments that appear in the standard 'T' flight instrument arrangement
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that the standard 'T' flight instrument arrangement places the airspeed indicator on the left, the attitude indicator on top center, the altimeter on the right, and the heading indicator on the bottom center of the top row, forming a 'T' shape with the attitude indicator at the top and the other three arranged around it.

flashcard.definition: The standard 'T' arrangement places: airspeed indicator (left), attitude indicator (top center), altimeter (right), and heading indicator (bottom center, directly below the attitude indicator). This forms a 'T' shape and is the FAA-preferred layout because the pilot's primary scan flows naturally around the attitude indicator.
flashcard.analogy: The 'T' puts the attitude indicator at the intersection, with airspeed, altitude, and heading radiating outward — the pilot's primary reference at the center.

jeopardy.question: These are the four instruments that appear in the standard 'T' flight instrument arrangement.
choices: Airspeed indicator, attitude indicator, altimeter, and heading indicator
Attitude indicator, heading indicator, turn coordinator, VSI
Altimeter, ASI, VSI, and turn coordinator
Attitude indicator, altimeter, magnetic compass, and engine tachometer
jeopardy.explanation: The 'T' arrangement places the airspeed indicator (left), attitude indicator (top center), altimeter (right), and heading indicator (bottom center). Other standard-six instruments (VSI and turn coordinator) are in the lower row but not in the 'T' itself.

multipleChoice.question: Which four instruments appear in the standard 'T' flight instrument arrangement?
choices: Airspeed indicator, attitude indicator, altimeter, and heading indicator
Attitude indicator, heading indicator, turn coordinator, and VSI
Altimeter, airspeed indicator, VSI, and turn coordinator
Attitude indicator, altimeter, magnetic compass, and engine tachometer
multipleChoice.explanation: The 'T' consists of ASI (left), attitude indicator (top center), altimeter (right), and heading indicator (bottom center). This layout puts the primary-scan instrument (attitude) at the center and allows a natural scan pattern. VSI and turn coordinator are part of the classic 'standard six' but are positioned below the 'T' proper.

practice.question: A new technician looking at a standard six-pack instrument panel sees a cross or 'T' shape formed by four of the six instruments. Which four are they?
choices: Airspeed indicator (upper left), attitude indicator (upper center), altimeter (upper right), and heading indicator (center below the attitude indicator) — the T-arrangement preferred by the FAA
Attitude indicator, VSI, heading indicator, and turn coordinator
The four engine gauges
VSI, turn coordinator, altimeter, and tachometer
practice.explanation: The 'T' arrangement is FAA-preferred because it supports a natural scan pattern centered on the attitude indicator. The other two standard-six instruments (VSI and turn coordinator) fill the bottom row but are not part of the 'T' itself.

preassessment.question: Which instruments form the standard 'T' arrangement?
choices: ASI, attitude indicator, altimeter, heading
AI, HI, turn coordinator, VSI
Altimeter, ASI, VSI, turn coordinator
AI, altimeter, compass, tachometer

questions.learningObjective: Identify which standard flight instrument is NOT driven by a gyroscope
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that the magnetic compass is NOT a gyroscopic instrument — it uses magnetic alignment to Earth's magnetic field — while the attitude indicator, heading indicator (directional gyro), and turn coordinator ARE gyroscopic.

flashcard.definition: The magnetic compass is NOT a gyroscopic instrument — it is a magnetic instrument that senses the horizontal component of Earth's magnetic field. The three traditional gyroscopic instruments are: attitude indicator, heading indicator (directional gyro), and turn coordinator/turn-and-bank indicator.
flashcard.analogy: Magnetic compass: passive, no moving power-driven parts. Gyros: spinning rotors powered by vacuum or electric.

jeopardy.question: This flight instrument is NOT typically driven by a gyroscope.
choices: Attitude indicator
Magnetic compass
Heading indicator (directional gyro)
Turn coordinator
jeopardy.explanation: The magnetic compass aligns to Earth's magnetic field — no gyroscope required. The attitude indicator, heading indicator, and turn coordinator are the three gyroscopic instruments.

multipleChoice.question: Which of the following flight instruments is NOT typically driven by a gyroscope?
choices: The attitude indicator
The magnetic compass
The heading indicator (directional gyro)
The turn coordinator
multipleChoice.explanation: The magnetic compass is a passive magnetic instrument — it senses the horizontal component of Earth's magnetic field via a pivoting magnet assembly. The other three listed instruments are gyroscopic: the attitude indicator uses a gyro to indicate pitch and roll, the heading indicator uses a gyro aligned to the compass for stable heading, and the turn coordinator uses a canted gyro to sense yaw rate.

practice.question: A pilot's checklist calls for verifying 'no gyroscopic instruments have unusual indications before flight.' Which instrument is the pilot NOT checking?
choices: The attitude indicator
The magnetic compass, which is not a gyroscopic instrument
The heading indicator
The turn coordinator
practice.explanation: The three gyro instruments (AI, HI, turn coordinator) are part of a 'gyro check.' The magnetic compass is checked separately — looking for correct reading, readability, and fluid clarity — but it is not part of the gyroscopic-instrument verification.

preassessment.question: Which instrument is NOT typically driven by a gyroscope?
choices: Attitude indicator
Magnetic compass
Heading indicator
Turn coordinator

questions.learningObjective: Describe what the Attitude and Heading Reference System (AHRS) in an EFIS uses solid-state sensors to determine
questions.fullObjective: Recognize that the AHRS in an EFIS uses solid-state sensors (typically MEMS gyros, accelerometers, and magnetometers) to determine aircraft attitude (pitch and roll) and heading, replacing traditional mechanical gyroscopic instruments.

flashcard.definition: The Attitude and Heading Reference System (AHRS) uses solid-state MEMS sensors — three-axis gyros, accelerometers, and magnetometers — to determine aircraft pitch, roll, and heading. The AHRS provides this data to the EFIS displays, replacing the mechanical gyroscopes used in traditional attitude and heading indicators.
